food_name: Penang Prawn Noodle Soup (Heh Mee) with Ramen Noodles
aliases: Penang Spicy Heh Mee Soup Ramen

english: Penang Spicy Heh Mee Soup Ramen

food_subgroup: Noodle soup dishes — prawn-based broth with ramen noodles

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Penang prawn noodle soup (Heh Mee). FOODON_00002473 (noodle soup food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP (Noodle soup) is the closest parent. No specific Penang prawn noodle or Heh Mee entry exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Penang prawn noodle soup or Heh Mee. SNOMED CT does not cover this regional dish at the required specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant bowl of Penang Heh Mee Ramen, approximately 500 g, comprising broth, ramen noodles, prawns, pork ribs, egg, and vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: Prawn noodle soup (Hokkien mee / Heh mee) — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA SR Legacy composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) Foodo; USDA Agricultural Research Service SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo lists Prawn Noodle Soup (Hokkien Mee / Heh Mee) as a reference dish. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Foodo's prawn noodle soup entry and cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy component data for ramen noodles and prawn broth. The ramen noodle substitution (in place of yellow wheat noodles) has minimal macronutrient impact. Values are expressed per 100 g of the combined dish (broth + noodles + toppings).

gi_source.title: Noodle soup dishes — GI estimation based on wheat noodle soup analogues; Atkinson FS et al. (2021) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values 2021
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Penang Heh Mee Ramen. GI estimated at 52 based on wheat noodle soup analogues in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021) International Tables of GI Values. Ramen noodles (alkaline wheat noodles) have a reported GI of approximately 50–55 in mixed soup dishes. The spicy prawn broth and protein content further moderate postprandial glucose response. GI 52 is assigned as a conservative low estimate. GL per serving = (52 × 42.5 g available carbohydrate per 500 g serving) / 100 = 22.1.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from prawn broth, shrimp paste (belacan), fish sauce, and soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: A single 500 g bowl provides approximately 2100 mg sodium, exceeding the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium broth, consume only half the soup, and avoid adding additional condiments such as soy sauce or chilli paste.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from ramen noodles, partially offset by high protein and broth volume
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 52 and a glycemic load of 22 per bowl, this dish poses a moderate postprandial glucose challenge; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider requesting a smaller noodle portion, pairing with additional vegetables, and monitoring blood glucose response, as the high-protein prawn and pork content helps moderate glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate cholesterol from prawns and pork, partially offset by low saturated fat per 100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium load (approximately 2100 mg per bowl) is the primary cardiovascular concern; saturated fat is moderate at 0.5 g per 100 g, and the prawn-based protein provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids, but individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size, avoid drinking the full broth, and choose this dish only occasionally as part of a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Penang Prawn Noodle Soup with Ramen Noodles (Heh Mee Ramen) is a Singaporean-served adaptation of the iconic Penang Hokkien prawn noodle dish, known locally as 'Heh Mee' (Hokkien for 'prawn noodles'). The dish features a deeply flavoured, slow-simmered broth made from prawn shells, prawn heads, pork ribs, dried shrimp, belacan (fermented shrimp paste), lemongrass, shallots, garlic, and chilli, producing a rich, umami-laden, spicy-savoury soup base. In this restaurant variant, ramen noodles (alkaline wheat noodles) replace the traditional yellow wheat noodles or rice vermicelli, lending a springy, chewy texture. The bowl is typically served with whole prawns, slices of pork ribs, a hard-boiled or soft-boiled egg, kangkong (water spinach) or bean sprouts, and a dollop of sambal chilli paste on the side. The flavour profile is intensely savoury, moderately spicy, and slightly sweet from the natural sweetness of prawn shells. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 68 kcal, 4.8 g protein, 1.8 g fat, and 8.5 g carbohydrate. The primary clinical concern is sodium: a full 500 g bowl delivers approximately 2100 mg sodium, making it a high-sodium meal that requires caution for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 52 (low), with a glycemic load of approximately 22 per bowl, representing a moderate glycaemic challenge for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with crustacean, gluten, egg, soy, or fish allergies. It is not reliably halal due to the inclusion of pork ribs. This dish is culturally significant as a heritage street food of Penang, Malaysia, and is widely served across Singapore in hawker centres, food courts, and casual dining restaurants.

ingredient_synonyms.ramen noodles: alkaline wheat noodles / springy wheat noodles

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Foodo's prawn noodle soup (Heh Mee / Hokkien Mee) reference entry and cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy component data. The ramen noodle substitution introduces minor uncertainty. GI is an analogous estimate from wheat noodle soup data in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021). No exact match for this specific restaurant variant exists in any approved primary source. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are per 100 g of the combined dish (broth, noodles, toppings) as served. A standard restaurant bowl is estimated at 500 g. 2. Sodium content is very high due to the prawn-based broth, belacan, fish sauce, and soy sauce; the per-100g value of 420 mg sodium reflects the diluted whole-bowl average. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a minimally processed broth-based dish with no hydrogenated fats. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy sources for this dish type. 5. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for prawn noodle soup. 6. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this dish. 7. GI is estimated at 52 based on wheat noodle soup analogues from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021); no direct GI measurement exists for Penang Heh Mee Ramen. 8. The dish is not reliably halal due to the inclusion of pork ribs in the traditional recipe; the restaurant variant (Flavours by Saute, Funan) should be individually verified for halal certification. 9. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy prawn and pork rib component data. 10. GL per serving calculated as: GI (52) × available carbohydrate per serving (42.5 g for 500 g bowl at 8.5 g/100g) / 100 = 22.1.
